NBA_model

Data exploration & cleaning

#Import dataset

NBA_Dataset <- read.csv("~/NBA_Dataset.csv")
print(NBA_Dataset[1:5,])
  season              player pos age team_id  g gs mp_per_g fg_per_g fga_per_g

So there is a bunch of NA’s, so we explore the data preliminarilly

str(NBA_Dataset)
'data.frame':   17697 obs. of  55 variables:
 $ season          : int  1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 1982 ...
 $ player          : chr  "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar" "Alvan Adams" "Mark Aguirre" "Danny Ainge" ...

There are columns with missing data, which can be separated into two categories:

  1. Percentages where the denominator is zero - players didn’t attempt that type of shot, so calculating a percentage would result in an error (undefined) -> fixed by replacing the NA’s with zero
  2. PER of zero - which we’ll investigate now
NBA_Dataset %>% filter(is.na(per))
  season        player pos age team_id g gs mp_per_g fg_per_g fga_per_g fg_pct
1   2006   Alex Scales  SG  27     SAS 1  0        0        0         0     NA
2   2010 JamesOn Curry  PG  24     LAC 1  0        0        0         0     NA
3   2013  Damion James  SF  25     BRK 2  0        0        0         0     NA
  fg3_per_g fg3a_per_g fg3_pct fg2_per_g fg2a_per_g fg2_pct efg_pct ft_per_g
1         0          0      NA         0          0      NA      NA        0
2         0          0      NA         0          0      NA      NA        0
3         0          0      NA         0          0      NA      NA        0
  fta_per_g ft_pct orb_per_g drb_per_g trb_per_g ast_per_g stl_per_g blk_per_g
1         0     NA         0       0.0       0.0         0         0         0
2         0     NA         0       0.0       0.0         0         0         0
3         0     NA         0       0.5       0.5         0         0         0
  tov_per_g pf_per_g pts_per_g mp per ts_pct fg3a_per_fga_pct fta_per_fga_pct
1         0        0         0  0  NA     NA               NA              NA
2         0        0         0  0  NA     NA               NA              NA
3         0        0         0  0  NA     NA               NA              NA
  orb_pct drb_pct trb_pct ast_pct stl_pct blk_pct tov_pct usg_pct ows dws ws
1      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA   0   0  0
2      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA   0   0  0
3      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA      NA   0   0  0
  ws_per_48 obpm dbpm  bpm vorp award_share   mov mov_adj win_loss_pct
1        NA -5.0 -0.7 -5.7    0           0  6.82    6.69        0.768
2        NA -6.4 -0.6 -7.0    0           0 -6.35   -6.01        0.354
3        NA -3.8 17.8 14.0    0           0  1.78    1.25        0.598

It is found that the NA’s for the PER are the result of players that played less then half a minute, so their minutes played was rounded to zero, which then made the PER undefined. Players with zero minutes were excluded from the dataset initially, so we just do that.

#Fixing the NA's

## To remove the na's for per
NBA_Dataset <- NBA_Dataset %>% filter(!is.na(NBA_Dataset$per))

## To remove the na's for the pct's
NBA_Dataset[is.na(NBA_Dataset)]=0

skim(NBA_Dataset)

pts_per_g 0 1 8.42 6.10 0.00 3.70 6.90 11.90 37.10 ▇▅▂▁▁
mp 0 1 1259.05 915.28 1.00 406.25 1177.00 2000.75 3533.00 ▇▅▅▃▁
per 0 1 12.78 6.15 -90.60 9.90 12.90 15.90 133.80 ▁▁▇▁▁
ts_pct 0 1 0.51 0.10 0.00 0.48 0.52 0.56 1.50 ▁▇▁▁▁
fg3a_per_fga_pct 0 1 0.19 0.21 0.00 0.01 0.12 0.34 1.00 ▇▃▂▁▁
fta_per_fga_pct 0 1 0.31 0.22 0.00 0.19 0.28 0.38 6.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
orb_pct 0 1 6.01 4.87 0.00 2.40 5.00 8.80 100.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
drb_pct 0 1 14.07 6.51 0.00 9.30 13.10 18.20 100.00 ▇▂▁▁▁
trb_pct 0 1 10.04 4.97 0.00 6.20 9.20 13.40 86.40 ▇▁▁▁▁
ast_pct 0 1 13.14 9.47 0.00 6.50 10.30 17.80 100.00 ▇▂▁▁▁
stl_pct 0 1 1.63 1.01 0.00 1.10 1.50 2.00 25.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
blk_pct 0 1 1.53 1.76 0.00 0.40 1.00 2.10 77.80 ▇▁▁▁▁
tov_pct 0 1 14.40 6.31 0.00 10.90 13.70 17.00 100.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
usg_pct 0 1 18.86 5.46 0.00 15.30 18.50 22.10 100.00 ▇▅▁▁▁
ows 0 1 1.37 2.10 -3.30 0.00 0.60 2.20 15.20 ▇▇▂▁▁
dws 0 1 1.29 1.22 -1.00 0.30 1.00 1.90 9.10 ▇▆▁▁▁
ws 0 1 2.66 3.02 -2.10 0.30 1.70 4.10 21.20 ▇▅▁▁▁
ws_per_48 0 1 0.07 0.10 -2.52 0.04 0.08 0.12 2.71 ▁▁▇▁▁
obpm 0 1 -1.49 3.99 -68.90 -3.20 -1.30 0.40 199.40 ▁▇▁▁▁
dbpm 0 1 -0.19 1.79 -31.10 -1.00 -0.20 0.70 60.70 ▁▇▁▁▁
bpm 0 1 -1.68 4.96 -92.10 -3.50 -1.40 0.50 242.20 ▁▇▁▁▁
vorp 0 1 0.64 1.37 -2.60 -0.10 0.10 1.00 12.50 ▇▅▁▁▁
award_share 0 1 0.01 0.06 0.00 0.00 0.00 0.00 1.00 ▇▁▁▁▁
mov 0 1 0.00 4.41 -15.20 -2.82 0.00 3.02 12.24 ▁▃▇▆▁
mov_adj 0 1 0.00 4.26 -14.68 -2.62 0.00 2.84 11.80 ▁▃▇▆▁
win_loss_pct 0 1 0.50 0.15 0.11 0.40 0.50 0.61 0.89 ▁▃▇▅▁

Incompleteness issues now fixed. Removed 3 records, so 17 694 remain. Dataset has 55 columns, of which 3 are categorical (player, pos, team_id) and the rest are numerical.

Regarding the categorical variables, the name is removed from the analysis (player name doesn’t matter), while the positions and team variables are converted to individual binary variables for each position and team.

# Conversion of pos & team_id to factors

NBA_Dataset$pos <- as.factor(NBA_Dataset$pos)
NBA_Dataset$team_id <- as.factor(NBA_Dataset$team_id)

levels(NBA_Dataset$team_id)
 [1] "ATL" "BOS" "BRK" "CHA" "CHH" "CHI" "CHO" "CLE" "DAL" "DEN" "DET" "GSW"
[13] "HOU" "IND" "KCK" "LAC" "LAL" "MEM" "MIA" "MIL" "MIN" "NJN" "NOH" "NOK"
[25] "NOP" "NYK" "OKC" "ORL" "PHI" "PHO" "POR" "SAC" "SAS" "SDC" "SEA" "TOR"
[37] "TOT" "UTA" "VAN" "WAS" "WSB"
levels(NBA_Dataset$pos)
 [1] "C"        "C-PF"     "PF"       "PF-C"     "PF-SF"    "PG"      
 [7] "PG-SF"    "PG-SG"    "SF"       "SF-C"     "SF-PF"    "SF-SG"   
[13] "SG"       "SG-PF"    "SG-PG"    "SG-PG-SF" "SG-SF"

Despite there being only 30 teams in the nba, the dataset has 41 teams. To allow for the 1-for-1 comparison, the older teams are replaced by either their new rebranded version, or replaced by the most similar, be it by location, shared history,etc.

#fixing team names

NBA_Dataset$team_id <- recode(NBA_Dataset$team_id,
  "CHH" = "CHA",
  "CHO" = "CHA",
  "KCK" = "SAC",
  "NJN" = "BRK",
  "NOH" = "NOP",
  "NOK" = "NOP",
  "SDC" = "LAC",
  "SEA" = "OKC",
  "TOT" = "CLE",
  "VAN" = "MEM",
  "WSB" = "WAS"
)


NBA_Dataset$team_id <- recode(NBA_Dataset$team_id,
  "1"  = "ATL",
  "9"  = "DAL",
  "10" = "DEN",
  "11" = "DET",
  "12" = "GSW",
  "13" = "HOU",
  "14" = "IND",
  "16" = "LAC",
  "17" = "LAL",
  "18" = "MEM",
  "19" = "MIA",
  "2"  = "BOS",
  "20" = "MIL",
  "21" = "MIN",
  "25" = "NOP",
  "26" = "NYK",
  "27" = "OKC",
  "28" = "NOP",
  "29" = "PHI",
  "3"  = "BRK",
  "30" = "PHO",
  "31" = "PHO",
  "32" = "SAC",
  "33" = "SAS",
  "36" = "TOR",
  "38" = "UTA",
  "40" = "WAS",
  "6"  = "CHI",
  "8"  = "CLE",
  "4"  = "CHA"
)

NBA_Dataset$team_id <- as.factor(NBA_Dataset$team_id)

levels(NBA_Dataset$team_id)
 [1] "ATL" "BOS" "BRK" "CHA" "CHI" "CLE" "DAL" "DEN" "DET" "GSW" "HOU" "IND"
[13] "SAC" "LAC" "LAL" "MEM" "MIA" "MIL" "MIN" "NOP" "NYK" "OKC" "ORL" "PHI"
[25] "PHO" "POR" "SAS" "TOR" "UTA" "WAS"

Changes are as follows:

  • CHH,CHO -> CHA

  • KCK -> SAC

  • NJN -> BRK

  • NOH,NOK -> NOP

  • SDC -> LAC

  • SEA -> OKC

  • TOT -> CLE

  • VAN -> MEM

  • WSB -> WAS

Now only the 30 current teams remain.

table(NBA_Dataset$pos)

       C     C-PF       PF     PF-C    PF-SF       PG    PG-SF    PG-SG 
    3579       31     3633       31       31     3371        1       33 
      SF     SF-C    SF-PF    SF-SG       SG    SG-PF    SG-PG SG-PG-SF 
    3291        1       24       39     3561        4       31        1 
   SG-SF 
      32

There are multiple counts of only 1-5, which will be problematic for modelling. Therefore these values are simplified to only list the 5 primary positions (the first one where there is an aggregation).

NBA_Dataset$pos <- recode(NBA_Dataset$pos,
                          "C-PF" = "C",
                          "PF-C" = "PF",
                          "PF-SF" = "PF",
                          "PG-SF" = "PG",
                          "PG-SG" = "PG",
                          "SF-C" = "SF",
                          "SF-PF" = "SF",
                          "SF-SG" = "SF",
                          "SG-PF" = "SG",
                          "SG-PG" = "SG",
                          "SG-PG-SF" = "SG",
                          "SG-SF" = "SG")

NBA_Dataset$pos <- as.factor(NBA_Dataset$pos)

levels(NBA_Dataset$pos)
[1] "C"  "PF" "PG" "SF" "SG"

This concludes all the preliminary data exploration and cleaning

The final step is to one-hot encode all the categorical variables:

encoded_team_id <- model.matrix( ~ team_id - 1, NBA_Dataset) 
colnames(encoded_team_id) <- c("ATL", "BOS", "BRK", "CHA", "CHI", "CLE", "DAL", "DEN", "DET", "GSW", "HOU", "IND", "SAC", "LAC", "LAL", "MEM", "MIA", "MIL", "MIN", "NOP", "NYK", "OKC", "ORL", "PHI", "PHO", "POR", "SAS", "TOR", "UTA", "WAS")

encoded_pos <- model.matrix( ~ pos - 1, NBA_Dataset)
colnames(encoded_pos) <- c("C","PF","PG","SF","SG")

NBA_Dataset <- cbind(NBA_Dataset,encoded_pos,encoded_team_id)

#To remove player names (not needed), position (encoded) and team id (encoded)
Data <- NBA_Dataset[,-c(2,3,5)]

The final dataset has 17 694 obs of 88 variables

EDA

Correlations

#Correlations
cor_data <- cor(Data)
cor.plot(cor_data, colors = TRUE)

There are correlations throughout for the volume related variable (2pt attempts and minutes/games played).

PCA

##Scree plot
fa.parallel(Data)
In smc, smcs < 0 were set to .0
In smc, smcs < 0 were set to .0
In smc, smcs < 0 were set to .0
Warning in cor.smooth(r): Matrix was not positive definite, smoothing was done
In factor.scores, the correlation matrix is singular, the pseudo inverse is  used
Parallel analysis suggests that the number of factors =  16  and the number of components =  14

This suggests 14 components

#PCA
PCA_nba <- PCA(Data, ncp = 14)
fviz_pca_biplot(PCA_nba)
Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.
ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the ggpubr package.
  Please report the issue at <https://github.com/kassambara/ggpubr/issues>.

The individual PCA plots brings to the forefront another issue with the dataset. There are instances of players that played a very small number of games, where great numbers have been put up without the volume. Although a fitted model would take this into account, it is better to remove these players altogether as they are unnecessary to the model and would only increase training time with no real upside.

Data cleaning 2

# Filter to keep only players that have played at least 25 games
Data <- Data %>% filter(Data$g >= 25)

After removing players that have played less than 20 games, the dataset now contains 14 405 obs and 87 variables.

EDA 2

Correlations 2

#Correlations
cor_data <- cor(Data)
cor.plot(Data)

PCA 2

#PCA for plots
PCA_nba <- PCA(Data, ncp = 14)
fviz_pca_biplot(PCA_nba)

The first PC accounting for 21.6% of variation seems to describe the general performance quality while the second PC accounting for 10.8% of variation describes the size of the player.

# PCA for loadings
pca_nba <- pca(Data, nfactors = 14)
Warning in cor.smooth(r): Matrix was not positive definite, smoothing was done
Warning in principal(r = r, nfactors = nfactors, residuals = residuals, : The
matrix is not positive semi-definite, scores found from Structure loadings
pca_nba$loadings

The first 14 PC’s only contain 58% of the variation of the original data. The PC’s are therefore not a good substitute.

Clustering

Optnum_kmeans <- fviz_nbclust(Data, kmeans, method = "wss")
Optnum_kmeans

The elbow bends after the 4th cluster, therefore four clusters are chosen for the clustering procedure.

Cluster_nba <- kmeans(Data, 4)

Cluster_plot <- fviz_cluster(Cluster_nba, data = Data, ellipse = TRUE, geom = c("point","text"))
Cluster_plot

The four clusters are separated along the 1st dimension, so the cluster are split by their “general performance quality”. Cluster 1 being the highest quality performers while cluster 2 is the lowest quality performers.

Linear Models

The team variable is removed, then to avoid multicollinearity issues, the following predictors are also removed:

  • mp_per_g

  • mp

  • fg_per_g

  • fga_per_g

  • fg2_pct

  • fg3a_per_g

  • fg2a_per_g

  • efg_pct

  • ft_per_g

  • fta_per_g

  • orb_per_g

  • drb_per_g

  • ast_pct

  • blk_pct

  • obpm

  • bpm

  • orb_pct

  • drb_pct

  • ows

  • dws

  • vorp

  • ws_per_48

  • mov_adj

  • mov

  • C

Normal regression (baseline)

norm_model <- lm(award_share ~ season + age + g + gs + fg_pct + fg3_per_g + fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + ast_per_g + stl_per_g + blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + pts_per_g + per + ts_pct + fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + SF + SG, data = Data)

summary(norm_model)

Call:
lm(formula = award_share ~ season + age + g + gs + fg_pct + fg3_per_g + 
    fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + ast_per_g + stl_per_g + 
    blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + pts_per_g + per + ts_pct + 
    fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + 
    tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + 
    SF + SG, data = Data)

trb_pct           0.00228608  0.00037729   6.059   0.0000000014026878 ***
stl_pct           0.00336744  0.00200351   1.681              0.09283 .  
tov_pct           0.00115906  0.00023567   4.918   0.0000008829983898 ***
usg_pct           0.00167320  0.00028291   5.914   0.0000000034100536 ***
ws                0.01696767  0.00051058  33.232 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
dbpm              0.00500064  0.00074222   6.737   0.0000000000167367 ***
win_loss_pct     -0.03499492  0.00421569  -8.301 < 0.0000000000000002 ***
PF                0.00092970  0.00165677   0.561              0.57470    
PG               -0.00407587  0.00307873  -1.324              0.18556    
SF                0.00125656  0.00218298   0.576              0.56488    
SG                0.00140327  0.00256907   0.546              0.58493    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.05634 on 14373 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2644,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2628 
F-statistic: 166.7 on 31 and 14373 DF,  p-value: < 0.00000000000000022

The metrics are as follows:

#Model metrics
norm_mse <- mse(Data$award_share, norm_model$fitted.values)
norm_aic <- AIC(norm_model)
norm_bic <- BIC(norm_model)
norm_Rsquared <- 1-((sum(norm_model$residuals^2))/(sum((Data$award_share-mean(Data$award_share))^2)))

norm_metrics <- as.table(cbind(norm_mse, norm_aic, norm_bic,norm_Rsquared)) 
colnames(norm_metrics) <- c("MSE","AIC","BIC","R-squared")
print(norm_metrics)
               MSE              AIC              BIC        R-squared
A      0.003167653 -41951.762657618 -41701.776746250      0.264418025

The R-squared is low so the normal linear model is not appropriate.

Gamma regression

Response values of zero return undefined gamma variable values so a transformation is applied to the response to ensure it lies between 0 & 1 as follows:

award_share_scaled01 <- scaleTR(Data$award_share, high = 1, low = 0, scale = 0.5)
summary(award_share_scaled01)
      Min.    1st Qu.     Median       Mean    3rd Qu.       Max. 
0.00003471 0.00003471 0.00003471 0.00743218 0.00003471 0.99996529

The award share now strictly lies between zero and one

gamma_model <- glm(award_share_scaled01 ~ season + age + g + gs + fg_pct + fg3_per_g + fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + ast_per_g + stl_per_g + blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + pts_per_g + per + ts_pct + fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + SF + SG, data = Data, family = Gamma(link = "inverse"))

Call:
glm(formula = award_share_scaled01 ~ season + age + g + gs + 
    fg_pct + fg3_per_g + fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + 
    ast_per_g + stl_per_g + blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + 
    pts_per_g + per + ts_pct + fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + 
    trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + 
    PF + PG + SF + SG, family = Gamma(link = "inverse"), data = Data)

(Dispersion parameter for Gamma family taken to be 558.0323)

    Null deviance: 145979  on 14404  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  54032  on 14373  degrees of freedom
AIC: -219746

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 22
gamma_mse <- rmse(Data$award_share, gamma_model$fitted.values)
gamma_aic <- AIC(gamma_model)
gamma_bic <- BIC(gamma_model)
gamma_Rsquared <- 1-((sum(gamma_model$residuals^2))/(sum((Data$award_share-mean(Data$award_share))^2)))

gamma_metrics <- as.table(cbind(gamma_mse, gamma_aic, gamma_bic,gamma_Rsquared)) 
colnames(gamma_metrics) <- c("MSE","AIC","BIC","R-squared")
print(gamma_metrics)
                     MSE                    AIC                    BIC
A              0.1434323        -219746.0651125        -219496.0792011
               R-squared
A -2311157962321.3896484

Despite an MSE lower than that of the baseline model, the r-squared is negative, a sign of a terrible fit - the variation of the model is bigger than the variation of the data, and the residuals plot confirming the model is not a good fit for the data.

Beta regression

#First you define the control
beta.control = betareg.control(phi = FALSE, hessian = TRUE, trace = TRUE)

beta_model <- betareg(award_share_scaled01 ~ season + age + g + gs + fg_pct + fg3_per_g + fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + ast_per_g + stl_per_g + blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + pts_per_g + per + ts_pct + fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + SF + SG, data = Data, dist = "beta", control = beta.control, link = "log")

Call:
betareg(formula = award_share_scaled01 ~ season + age + g + gs + fg_pct + 
    fg3_per_g + fg3_pct + fg2_per_g + ft_pct + trb_per_g + ast_per_g + 
    stl_per_g + blk_per_g + tov_per_g + pf_per_g + pts_per_g + per + 
    ts_pct + fg3a_per_fga_pct + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + 
    tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + dbpm + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + SF + SG, 
    data = Data, link = "log", dist = "beta", control = beta.control)

Coefficients (mean model with log link):
     (Intercept)            season               age                 g  
      -2.2403457        -0.0007796        -0.0003107        -0.0045436  
              gs            fg_pct         fg3_per_g           fg3_pct

beta_rmse <- rmse(Data$award_share, beta_model$fitted.values)
beta_aic <- AIC(beta_model)
beta_bic <- BIC(beta_model)
beta_Rsquared <- 1-((sum(beta_model$residuals^2))/(sum((Data$award_share-mean(Data$award_share))^2)))

beta_metrics <- as.table(cbind(beta_rmse, beta_aic, beta_bic,beta_Rsquared)) 
colnames(beta_metrics) <- c("MSE","AIC","BIC","R-squared")
round(beta_metrics,6)
            MSE           AIC           BIC     R-squared
A  6.054500e-02 -1.980561e+05 -1.978061e+05  1.489920e-01

This model is outperformed by the baseline for all metrics.

Generalised Additive Model

GA_Model <- gamlss(award_share_scaled01 ~ season + pb(age) + pb(g) + gs + fg_pct + pb(fg3_per_g) + pb(fg3_pct) + fg2_per_g + pb(ft_pct) + pb(trb_per_g) + pb(ast_per_g) + pb(stl_per_g) + pb(blk_per_g) + pb(tov_per_g) + pb(pf_per_g) + pts_per_g + per + pb(ts_pct) + pb(fg3a_per_fga_pct) + fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct + usg_pct + ws + pb(dbpm) + win_loss_pct + PF + PG + SF + SG, data = Data, family = BE())

print(GA_Model)

Family:  c("BE", "Beta") 
Fitting method: RS() 

Call:  gamlss(formula = award_share_scaled01 ~ season + pb(age) +  
    pb(g) + gs + fg_pct + pb(fg3_per_g) + pb(fg3_pct) +  
    fg2_per_g + pb(ft_pct) + pb(trb_per_g) + pb(ast_per_g) +  
    pb(stl_per_g) + pb(blk_per_g) + pb(tov_per_g) +  
    pb(pf_per_g) + pts_per_g + per + pb(ts_pct) + pb(fg3a_per_fga_pct) +  
    fta_per_fga_pct + trb_pct + stl_pct + tov_pct +  
    usg_pct + ws + pb(dbpm) + win_loss_pct + PF + PG +  
    SF + SG, family = BE(), data = Data) 

Mu Coefficients:

Global Deviance:     -198991 
            AIC:     -198688 
            SBC:     -197540 
GA_mse <- mse(Data$award_share, GA_Model$y)
GA_aic <- AIC(GA_Model)
GA_bic <- BIC(GA_Model)
GA_mae <- mae(Data$award_share, GA_Model$y)
GA_rmsle <- rmsle(Data$award_share, GA_Model$y)
GA_Rsquared <- 1-((sum(GA_Model$residuals^2))/(sum((Data$award_share-mean(Data$award_share))^2)))
GA_metrics <- as.table(cbind(GA_mse, GA_aic, GA_bic,GA_mae, GA_rmsle, GA_Rsquared))
colnames(GA_metrics) <- c("MSE","AIC","BIC","MAE","RMSLE","R-squared")
print(GA_metrics)
            MSE           AIC           BIC           MAE         RMSLE
A  1.190160e-09 -1.986880e+05 -1.975397e+05  3.441434e-05  3.444389e-05
      R-squared
A          -Inf

Despite the MSE being very close to zero, the r-squared is undefined. This is a sign of overfitting.

Feature selection

This part will make use of random forest and lasso regression to determine the most insignificant features to be removed.

The first step is to scale the features, then split the dataset into the features and the response:

#To split into the features and response

#scale the features

Data_scaled <- scale(Data)

#To split into the features and response

X <- (as.data.frame(Data_scaled)) %>% select(c("season", "age", "g", "gs", "fg_pct", "fg3_per_g", "fg3_pct", "fg2_per_g", "ft_pct", "trb_per_g", "ast_per_g", "stl_per_g", "blk_per_g", "tov_per_g", "pf_per_g", "pts_per_g", "per", "ts_pct", "fg3a_per_fga_pct", "fta_per_fga_pct", "trb_pct", "stl_pct", "tov_pct", "usg_pct", "ws", "dbpm", "win_loss_pct", "PF", "PG", "SF", "SG"))

Y <- Data$award_share

Random Forest

#rf model fitting
rf_model <- randomForest(x = X, y = Y, replace = TRUE, nodesize = 3, keep.inbag = TRUE, nPerm = 10 )

print(rf_model)

Call:
 randomForest(x = X, y = Y, replace = TRUE, nodesize = 3, nPerm = 10,      keep.inbag = TRUE) 
               Type of random forest: regression
                     Number of trees: 500
No. of variables tried at each split: 10

          Mean of squared residuals: 0.001205569
                    % Var explained: 72
#Metrics

rf_mae <- mae(Data$award_share, rf_model$predicted)

rf_rmse <- rmse(Data$award_share, rf_model$predicted)

rf_rmsle <- rmsle(Data$award_share, rf_model$predicted)

rf_Rsquared <- 1-((sum((Data$award_share-rf_model$predicted)^2))/(sum((Data$award_share-mean(Data$award_share))^2)))

rf_metrics <- as.table(cbind(rf_rmse, rf_mae, rf_rmsle, rf_Rsquared))
colnames(rf_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE","R-squared")

round(rf_metrics,6)
      RMSE      MAE    RMSLE R-squared
A 0.034721 0.005315 0.025887  0.720047

There is a big improvement for the R-squared compared to the linear models.

# Extracting feature importance from the random forest

rf_importance <- as.data.frame(rf_model$importance)
rf_importance <-as.data.frame(cbind(as.data.frame(rownames(rf_importance)),rf_importance$IncNodePurity))
colnames(rf_importance) <- c("Feature","NodePurity")

rf_importance <- rf_importance %>% arrange(desc(NodePurity))
print(rf_importance)
            Feature  NodePurity
1                ws 18.21711953
2               per 11.72706623
3      win_loss_pct  4.84751533
4         pts_per_g  4.53726195
5           usg_pct  2.94292729
6         fg2_per_g  1.94366463
7              dbpm  1.83484085
8         tov_per_g  1.48358701
9         ast_per_g  1.46331371
10        trb_per_g  0.90658233
11           ts_pct  0.87855935
12               gs  0.85509345
13  fta_per_fga_pct  0.82766486
14                g  0.81417099
15           ft_pct  0.80990793
16           fg_pct  0.72186302
17         pf_per_g  0.71340505
18           season  0.68113426
19          tov_pct  0.62359308
20          trb_pct  0.61883091
21              age  0.58776772
22        blk_per_g  0.56109653
23 fg3a_per_fga_pct  0.54204440
24          fg3_pct  0.53198697
25        stl_per_g  0.52302023
26        fg3_per_g  0.45609668
27          stl_pct  0.44636112
28               PF  0.09772656
29               SF  0.05679087
30               SG  0.05036862
31               PG  0.03771166

The most useful predictors are the win shares, PER, win percentage and the pts per game. Usage, 2pt field goals per game, dbpm, assists per game and turnovers per game also all have node purities greater than one.

The least useful predictors are the position, defensive counting stats (blocks/steals per game) and age.

Lasso Regression

lasso_model <- glmnet(x = X, y = Y, alpha = 1)

print(lasso_model)

Call:  glmnet(x = X, y = Y, alpha = 1) 

   Df  %Dev    Lambda

print(lasso_coeff)
32 x 1 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
                    s=0.003084
(Intercept)       7.397987e-03
season            .

PF                .           
PG                .           
SF                .           
SG                .

Here you can see the varying impacts of each predictor, by finding the lambda that minimises some loss function (like mse loss).

Non-linear models

Bagged CART model

train_control <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10, savePredictions = "all", timingSamps = 1, returnResamp = "all")


CART_model <- train(X, Y, method = "treebag", metric = "RMSE", trControl = train_control, tuneLength = 3)

print(CART_model)
Bagged CART 

14405 samples
   31 predictor

No pre-processing
Resampling: Cross-Validated (10 fold) 
Summary of sample sizes: 12965, 12965, 12964, 12965, 12964, 12964, ... 
Resampling results:

  RMSE        Rsquared   MAE        
  0.03669043  0.6807102  0.005904535

Parallel random forest

# Parallel backend to run trees parallel instead of sequentially
num_cores <- parallel::detectCores() - 1

cl <- makeCluster(num_cores)
registerDoParallel(cl)

# Train control
train_control <- trainControl(method = "oob", savePredictions = "all", timingSamps = 1, returnResamp = "all")

# Model training
prf_model <- train(X, Y, method = "parRF", metric = "RMSE", trControl = train_control, tuneLength = 3)

#To stop cluster
stopCluster(cl)

print(prf_model)
Parallel Random Forest 

14405 samples
   31 predictor

No pre-processing
Resampling results across tuning parameters:

  mtry  RMSE        Rsquared 
   2    0.03819775  0.6611800
  16    0.03477684  0.7191504
  31    0.03520543  0.7121853

RMSE was used to select the optimal model using the smallest value.
The final value used for the model was mtry = 16.

Model testing

This compares the random forest, parallel random forest, cart, beta regression and baseline regression.

# Test dataset
NBA_test_dataset <- read_excel("C:/Users/Tsepo/Downloads/NBA_test_dataset.xlsx")


pre_scale_data <- (as.data.frame(Data)) %>% select(c("season", "age", "g", "gs", "fg_pct", "fg3_per_g", "fg3_pct", "fg2_per_g", "ft_pct", "trb_per_g", "ast_per_g", "stl_per_g", "blk_per_g", "tov_per_g", "pf_per_g", "pts_per_g", "per", "ts_pct", "fg3a_per_fga_pct", "fta_per_fga_pct", "trb_pct", "stl_pct", "tov_pct", "usg_pct", "ws", "dbpm", "win_loss_pct", "PF", "PG", "SF", "SG"))

complete_data <- rbind(pre_scale_data,NBA_test_dataset[,-c(1,33)])

complete_scaled <- scale(complete_data)

X_test <- complete_scaled[14406:14414,1:31]

Y_test <- NBA_test_dataset$award_share

Normal regression

#Normal regression test metrics

norm_predicts <- predict(norm_model,as.data.frame(X_test))

norm_mae <- mae(Y_test, norm_predicts)

norm_rmse <- rmse(Y_test, norm_predicts)

norm_rmsle <- rmsle(Y_test, norm_predicts)

norm_metrics <- as.table(cbind(norm_rmse, norm_mae, norm_rmsle))
colnames(norm_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE")

print(norm_metrics)
       RMSE       MAE     RMSLE
A 0.7959619 0.7198923 0.8815881

The normal regression, which had a training RMSE of close to zero, has produced a much ballooned RMSE this time around. The model was too simple and the assumptions of normality does not hold to the underlying structure of the data, so despite the good initial fit, the model was terrible when dealing with new data.

Beta regression

#Beta regression test metrics

beta_predicts <- predict(beta_model,as.data.frame(X_test))

beta_mae <- mae(Y_test, beta_predicts)

beta_rmse <- rmse(Y_test, beta_predicts)

beta_rmsle <- rmsle(Y_test, beta_predicts)

beta_metrics <- as.table(cbind(beta_rmse, beta_mae, beta_rmsle))
colnames(beta_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE")

print(beta_metrics)
       RMSE       MAE     RMSLE
A 0.4380176 0.2861607 0.3275465

The rmse is close to half that of the baseline, showing great improvement. However, the training rmse that was initially close to zero result in a greatly increased test rmse of 0.438, again a sign of a model that fails to comprehend the underlying structure of the data.

Random forest

# Random forest test metrics

rf_predicts <- predict(rf_model, X_test)

rf_mae <- mae(Y_test, rf_predicts)

rf_rmse <- rmse(Y_test, rf_predicts)

rf_rmsle <- rmsle(Y_test, rf_predicts)

rf_metrics <- as.table(cbind(rf_rmse, rf_mae, rf_rmsle))
colnames(rf_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE")

print(rf_metrics)
       RMSE       MAE     RMSLE
A 0.2173564 0.1772744 0.1497688

The random forest provides another improvement, reducing the test error by about 50% from the beta regression, and a 75% decrease from the baseline. This implies the data is non-linear. There is a relatively large increase from the training to the test rmse, a sign of overfitting.

CART

# CART model test metrics  

cart_predicts <- predict(CART_model, X_test)  

cart_mae <- mae(Y_test, cart_predicts)  
cart_rmse <- rmse(Y_test, cart_predicts)  
cart_rmsle <- rmsle(Y_test, cart_predicts)  

cart_metrics <- as.table(cbind(cart_rmse, cart_mae, cart_rmsle)) 
colnames(cart_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE")  

print(cart_metrics)
       RMSE       MAE     RMSLE
A 0.2403168 0.1745779 0.1629367

This model has slightly higher errors than the random forest, so that remains the best model.

Parallel random forest

# parallel random forest test metrics

prf_predicts <- predict(prf_model,X_test)

prf_mae <- mae(Y_test, prf_predicts)

prf_rmse <- rmse(Y_test, prf_predicts)

prf_rmsle <- rmsle(Y_test, prf_predicts)

prf_metrics <- as.table(cbind(prf_rmse, prf_mae, prf_rmsle))
colnames(prf_metrics) <- c("RMSE","MAE","RMSLE")

print(prf_metrics)
       RMSE       MAE     RMSLE
A 0.2095938 0.1683039 0.1435494

Despite initially having a higher training rmse than the random forest, this model has reduced the test error of the random forest by about 33%. The drop-off from the training to test rmse is also the smallest thus far. As the model with the lowest test metrics, the parallel random forest is the best model for predicting the award share.
